raw_data:
b' Money Stuff

VIEs

You can get a good sense of the differences between American and Chinese financial regulation from the recent troubles of Chinese tech companies listed in the U.S. Some Chinese companies in the internet, ride-sharing, education-technology, etc. sectors have listed in New York, and Chinese regulators have become unhappy about these companies and have reacted by doing things like restricting downloads of\xc2\xa0those companies\xe2\x80\x99 apps, or forbidding them from making profits . This has been bad for the companies\xe2\x80\x99 stock prices, for what I hope are obvious discounted-cash-flow-related reasons.

I do not have any special insight into why Chinese regulators are doing these things, though there are stated reasons \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0China worries about data security at tech companies, it worries about the social downsides of expensive high-stakes tutoring, etc. \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0that are plausible enough. And here\xe2\x80\x99s Ray Dalio :

In this case the policy makers signaled to DiDi that it might not be best to go ahead with the listing and they understandably want to deal with the data privacy issue. In the case of the educational tutoring companies they want to reduce the educational inequality and the financial burden on those who are desperate to have their children have these services but can\xe2\x80\x99t afford them by making them broadly available. They believe that these things are better for the country even if the shareholders don\xe2\x80\x99t like it. \xe2\x80\xa6

To understand what\xe2\x80\x99s going on you need to understand that China is a state capitalist system which means that the state runs capitalism to serve the interests of most people and that policy makers won\xe2\x80\x99t let the sensitivities of those in the capital markets and rich capitalists stand in the way of doing what they believe is best for the most people of the country. Rather, those in the capital markets and capitalists have to understand their subordinate places in the system or they will suffer the consequences of their mistakes. For example, they need to not mistake their having riches for having power for determining how things will go.

U.S. securities regulators, meanwhile, are in the investor-protection business, and they are unhappy that these Chinese stocks (1) were sold to U.S. investors and (2)\xc2\xa0went down. But their main, almost only, regulatory tool is disclosure. So when they see a broad swath of Chinese companies\xe2\x80\x99 stocks go down for reasons like \xe2\x80\x9ca whole sector is not allowed to make profits anymore,\xe2\x80\x9d they naturally think \xe2\x80\x9cwell this is a risk that should be disclosed.\xe2\x80\x9d

On the one hand that is a natural thing for them to think. On the other hand it is awkward because \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0that was a risk that was pretty well disclosed? Like a notable fact about Chinese tech companies listing in the U.S. is that they all disclose pretty clear risk factors to the effect of \xe2\x80\x9cwe are a Chinese tech company, we are not actually allowed to be owned by foreign investors, we\xe2\x80\x99ve got this rickety structure to get around the rules, we think it works but no one is really sure, and if it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t work all sorts of terrible things can happen and you\xe2\x80\x99ll never get your profits.\xe2\x80\x9d The rickety structure is called a \xe2\x80\x9cvariable interest entity,\xe2\x80\x9d we talked about it last month ,\xc2\xa0and it is all pretty well understood and disclosed.\xc2\xa0Obviously those risks are (were) ignored a lot, but that is not really a failure of disclosure.

But the Securities and Exchange Commission does what it does, so here is a \xe2\x80\x9c Statement on Investor Protection Related to Recent Developments in China \xe2\x80\x9d that SEC Chair Gary Gensler put out\xc2\xa0last week:

In a number of sectors in China, companies are not allowed to have foreign ownership and cannot directly list on exchanges outside of China. To raise money on such exchanges, many China-based operating companies are structured as Variable Interest Entities (VIEs). \xe2\x80\xa6

For U.S. investors, this arrangement creates \xe2\x80\x9cexposure\xe2\x80\x9d to the China-based operating company, though only through a series of service contracts and other contracts. To be clear, though, neither the investors in the shell company\xe2\x80\x99s stock, nor the offshore shell company itself, has stock ownership in the China-based operating company. I worry that average investors may not realize that they hold stock in a shell company rather than a China-based operating company.

In light of the recent developments in China and the overall risks with the China-based VIE structure, I have asked staff to seek certain disclosures from offshore issuers associated with China-based operating companies before their registration statements will be declared effective. In particular, I have asked staff to ensure that these issuers prominently and clearly disclose:

That investors are not buying shares of a China-based operating company but instead are buying shares of a shell company issuer that maintains service agreements with the associated operating company. Thus, the business description of the issuer should clearly distinguish the description of the shell company\xe2\x80\x99s management services from the description of the China-based operating company;

That the China-based operating company, the shell company issuer, and investors face uncertainty about future actions by the government of China that could significantly affect the operating company\xe2\x80\x99s financial performance and the enforceability of the contractual arrangements; and

Detailed financial information, including quantitative metrics, so that investors can understand the financial relationship between the VIE and the issuer.

But those disclosures already exist in every Chinese VIE offering. I quoted some of them last month when we talked about the initial public offering of Didi Global Inc., which ran into trouble with Chinese regulators almost immediately after it went public in New York. \xe2\x80\x9cThere are substantial uncertainties regarding the interpretation and application of current or future PRC laws and regulations,\xe2\x80\x9d said Didi\xe2\x80\x99s prospectus, about the VIE structure. And if anything changed, it said, \xe2\x80\x9cthe relevant governmental authorities would have broad discretion in dealing with such violation, including, without limitation: \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0revoking the business licenses and/or operating licenses of our PRC entities; \xe2\x80\xa6 [or] requiring us to restructure our ownership structure or operations, including terminating the contractual arrangements with our VIEs.\xe2\x80\x9d

Now, I suppose tweaks are still possible. For instance Gensler says that \xe2\x80\x9cthe business description of the issuer should clearly distinguish the description of the shell company\xe2\x80\x99s management services from the description of the China-based operating company,\xe2\x80\x9d and most Chinese VIE prospectuses arguably do not do that. \xe2\x80\x9cOur journey started on the streets of Beijing,\xe2\x80\x9d begins the founders\xe2\x80\x99 letter at the start of Didi\xe2\x80\x99s prospectus. The business section begins, \xe2\x80\x9cOur mission is to make life better by transforming mobility.\xe2\x80\x9d The prospectus\xc2\xa0says those things because it is describing a large Chinese ride-sharing company, and ride-sharing companies like to make grandiose claims about changing the world by letting you call a car with an app.

But technically the prospectus was not\xc2\xa0selling shares of that ride-sharing startup to U.S. investors; it was selling shares of a shell company with certain management contracts with that startup. \xe2\x80\x9cOur journey started in a Cayman Islands law firm,\xe2\x80\x9d a Gensler-approved founders\xe2\x80\x99 letter might start. \xe2\x80\x9cWe were united by a single dream: Being able to sell shares in a U.S.-listed company that has economic exposure to the Chinese ride-sharing business that we also happen to have founded, while complying with our lawyers\xe2\x80\x99 current interpretation of Chinese laws limiting foreign ownership of certain technology businesses.\xe2\x80\x9d And then the whole prospectus would be like that, constantly emphasizing that you are buying shares of a Cayman Islands shell company rather than the underlying Chinese business. And it would be more jarring to read than the actual Didi prospectus, which understandably focuses on the operating business rather than the legal structure.

And I suppose that is Gensler\xe2\x80\x99s goal, to make the prospectus more jarring , so that people who read it would be less likely to invest in the Chinese companies in the first place. I do not know if that's a good goal; my assumption is that the people who bother to read prospectuses already knew all of this and decided that Chinese VIE risk was acceptable, while the people who didn\xe2\x80\x99t know all this are not\xc2\xa0going to read a prospectus anyway. But it might have an effect. If the first sentence of the prospectus was \xe2\x80\x9cwe are a Cayman Islands shell company providing certain miscellaneous services to a Chinese tech company,\xe2\x80\x9d you could imagine journalists saying \xe2\x80\x9ca Cayman Islands shell company that provides certain miscellaneous services to a Chinese tech company is going public today,\xe2\x80\x9d and you could imagine retail investors hearing that and saying \xe2\x80\x9coh that doesn\xe2\x80\x99t sound very interesting\xe2\x80\x9d and passing.

You could imagine a stronger approach of, for instance, banning U.S.\xc2\xa0listings by Chinese VIE companies. (The SEC does seem to have paused them until the disclosure is better.) That would keep U.S. institutional investors out, too, so they couldn\xe2\x80\x99t lose money if China changes its rules. But that has obvious downsides (they can\xe2\x80\x99t make money either, etc.) and\xc2\xa0is not really how the SEC operates. It\xe2\x80\x99s just going to keep cranking up the disclosure requirements to make its displeasure clearer and clearer. But what you do with that displeasure is up to you. The Chinese approach is rather more drastic.

Elsewhere, I guess a lot of investors who lobbied index providers to increase the weighting of China in emerging-market indexes are sad now :

An indexing boom that\xe2\x80\x99s gone on for over a decade has coincided with China\xe2\x80\x99s emergence as the world\xe2\x80\x99s second-biggest economy, a confluence that\xe2\x80\x99s driven trillions of dollars into its burgeoning equity market -- often via passive funds.

These products track gauges like the MSCI Emerging Markets Index, where the weighting for mainland- and Hong Kong-based stocks has doubled in 10 years to about 35%. Or the MSCI Asia Pacific Index, where their shares account for over a quarter of the market value of all members.

In other words, the passive era has put untold billions at the mercy of Beijing\xe2\x80\x99s zeal for reform.

But that\xe2\x80\x99s the point of indexing, really; you get exposure to the world\xe2\x80\x99s markets, and everything that they (and their regulators, etc.) do. It would be good to have an index that included only stocks from markets that go up, and not ones that go down, but that\xe2\x80\x99s hard to get.

Short ARK

Let\xe2\x80\x99s say that you think celebrity stock-picker Cathie Wood is bad at picking stocks, and you want to bet against her. Specifically you think that her flagship actively managed\xc2\xa0exchange-traded fund, the ARK Innovation ETF (ticker ARKK), will go down, and you would like to make money when it does. How would you do that? One thing you could do is short all the stocks that she buys. You could do that reasonably effectively: ARK is an actively managed ETF, meaning that it changes its holdings unpredictably from time to time, but it does seem to do a pretty good job of disclosing its trading in something like real time. And it\xe2\x80\x99s not like she\xe2\x80\x99s in a high-frequency trading business; she\xe2\x80\x99s betting on broad economic themes rather than minute-by-minute trading dynamics. You could probably get a reasonable inverse of her fund by just watching what she trades and doing the opposite.

Still it is more work than the obvious trade. The obvious trade is: ARKK is an exchange-traded fund, which means you can buy and sell shares of the fund on the stock exchange, so you should just sell the fund short. Instead of betting against her by shorting the stocks she buys, just short the shares of her fund. Then every time her fund goes down you will make money, and you never need to adjust your trades or pay attention to her trades. You are automatically betting against whatever she does.

This is a standard nice feature of ETFs: If you want to bet on the ETF\xe2\x80\x99s theme or index or manager, you can buy the ETF, but if you want to bet against it you can sell the ETF short. The ETF as a product appeals to both sets of people, people who\xc2\xa0like some theme and people who want to bet against it. (The ETF only gets management fees from\xc2\xa0the former group\xc2\xa0though.) And in fact Bloomberg tells me that there are about 19.3 million shares of short interest on ARKK, or about $2.3 billion worth of shorts. (Versus about $22.8 billion of longs.) So lots of people do want to bet against Cathie Wood\xe2\x80\x99s portfolio and stock-picking skills, and do, in a straightforward way.

Still some ETFs come in two flavors, regular and inverse, where the inverse one generally goes up X% whenever the regular one goes down X%. And\xc2\xa0if you want to bet against the theme of the regular one you can short it, or you can buy the inverse one. I suppose this is more convenient or less scary for some investors than short selling is. There are also some nice weird synergies for the company offering the ETFs. For one thing, the longs and shorts both pay management fees. Also I suppose you can write a swap on the underlying thing between the long and short funds, reducing the need to actually hold (and short) the underlying thing.

You don\xe2\x80\x99t really see inverse funds\xc2\xa0with actively managed stock-picker ETFs, for what I think are kind of obvious reasons. (If you\xe2\x80\x99re offering that sort of ETF it\xe2\x80\x99s because you think you're a good stock-picker ; you\xe2\x80\x99re not indifferently looking to give people exposure either way.) But that doesn\xe2\x80\x99t mean that someone else can\xe2\x80\x99t do it :

Those who think Cathie Wood\xe2\x80\x99s hot hand is cooling may soon be able to express that view via an exchange-traded fund.

The Short ARKK ETF would seek to track the inverse performance of the $23 billion Ark Innovation ETF (ticker ARKK) -- the largest fund in Ark Investment Management\xe2\x80\x99s lineup -- through swaps contracts, according to a filing Friday with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission. The fund would trade under the ticker SARK and charge a 0.75% operating expense, in line with ARKK\xe2\x80\x99s fee. ...

SARK would be managed by Matt Tuttle, chief executive officer at Tuttle Capital Management LLC, an issuer of thematic and actively-managed ETFs.

\xe2\x80\x9cIn sum, as ARKK already represents a long exposure to a basket of unprofitable tech stocks, we thought that investors should have access to the short side as well,\xe2\x80\x9d Tuttle wrote in an email. \xe2\x80\x9cKeep in mind there are a lot of non institutional investors, that cannot short stocks or ETFs or they may have trouble finding a borrow to put on the short.\xe2\x80\x9d

Rude! Mostly this strikes me as a clever marketing\xc2\xa0move; surely there are some people who think \xe2\x80\x9cCathie Wood is over-hyped\xe2\x80\x9d but who would not go so far as to short her fund, but maybe if someone pitches them an anti-Cathie Wood fund they\xe2\x80\x99ll bite? I dunno. Anyway here's how that will work :

The Fund will enter into one or more swap agreements with major global financial institutions for a specified period ranging from a day to more than one year whereby the Fund and the global financial institution will agree to exchange the return (or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on the ETF.

If you are an ETF arbitrageur, it is nice to be able to get long ARKK in many different ways. You can buy the underlying portfolio, or you can buy shares of the ETF, or, now, you can write a swap to SARK and get long ARKK that way. And then your job is to get long the cheapest way and short the most expensive way, so that you are perfectly hedged and make a little profit; Cathie Wood and Matt Tuttle can argue who\xe2\x80\x99s right about stocks, but the arbs make money either way.

Elsewhere: \xe2\x80\x9cCathie Wood Is Just a Start as Stock Pickers Storm the ETF World .\xe2\x80\x9d

Sponsored Content

84% of ultra-high-net-worth individuals collect art. It makes sense \xe2\x80\x94contemporary art appreciated 13.6% per year over the last 25 years vs. 8.9% returns for the S&P 500.

Masterworks has fractionalized investments in masterpieces by Basquiat and Banksy. If you're looking for a tangible, non-correlated asset class, check out Masterworks today .\xc2\xa0\xc2\xa0(See Disclaimer )

Masterworks

Goldman pay

I dunno man :

Goldman Sachs Group Inc. raised pay for its junior investment bankers, among the last of the big Wall Street banks to do so following a presentation by the lender\xe2\x80\x99s own analysts revealing their crushing workloads.

First-year analysts at Goldman Sachs will now earn at least $110,000, according to people familiar with the matter. Their second-year counterparts will be paid $125,000, the people said, asking not to be identified discussing a private matter. Pay for first year associates will go to $150,000, they said.

Goldman held out so long on the theory that , as one Goldman person put it last month, \xe2\x80\x9cIf you behave like that [raising salaries] you simply end up with mercenaries.\xe2\x80\x9d Goldman\xe2\x80\x99s thing was, we are Goldman, we are special, you don\xe2\x80\x99t come here to maximize your first-year-analyst\xe2\x80\x99s salary, but if you come here and perform well you\xe2\x80\x99ll have special opportunities and get especially rich in due time. We don\xe2\x80\x99t want the people who want to maximize their first-year salaries; we want the special people. \xe2\x80\x9c Long-term greedy .\xe2\x80\x9d

That is, in the abstract, a perfectly plausible theory to have, and it works pretty well for lots of tech startups. But if you are going to have that theory you have to be sure it\xe2\x80\x99ll work for you too? Otherwise you just end up sheepishly raising salaries a month later, and the clear implication is that you couldn\xe2\x80\x99t attract enough of the special people with the lower salaries, because they don\xe2\x80\x99t think you\xe2\x80\x99re special enough. Now you\xe2\x80\x99re just looking for mercenaries.

Disclosure, I used to work at Goldman, back when it was special I guess.

Bored(om) market hypothesis

Last spring I proposed what I usually called the \xe2\x80\x9c boredom markets hypothesis .\xe2\x80\x9d As I once put it : \xe2\x80\x9ca lot of individual investors buy stocks mainly because it\xe2\x80\x99s fun, and ... the more fun stocks are, and the less fun everything else is , the more they\xe2\x80\x99ll buy stocks. In a pandemic, when people can\xe2\x80\x99t really leave their house and sports are canceled, there is a lot less fun to be had elsewhere, so trading stocks seems relatively more fun, so people buy more stocks.\xe2\x80\x9d

This was not especially rigorous or anything but, you know, look around. The stock market didn\xe2\x80\x99t seem especially rigorous. Stocks were rallying because they went bankrupt. Much of that worked out fine actually , and the bored retail investors\xc2\xa0often did pretty well, but at the time it felt really weird. It didn\xe2\x80\x99t seem like they were doing rigorous fundamental analysis, a lot of the time. It seemed like they would rather have been watching sports, but there were no sports, so they got their entertainment by trading stocks.

Anyway Richard Thaler won a whole Nobel Prize for analyzing why stock markets do dumb things, and he agrees. Here\xe2\x80\x99s an interview that he gave to a Swiss journalist published last week:

Professor Thaler, what do you make of current events in the stock markets? As a pioneer in the field of behavioral finance, these must be pretty exciting times.

I sometimes play golf with my colleague Gene Fama, and if we were keeping score by what\xe2\x80\x99s going on in the market rather than strokes made, I think I would be winning. Part of what seems to be going on I call the \xe2\x80\x9cbored market hypothesis,\xe2\x80\x9d because during the pandemic when people were working at home, especially in the beginning, they just had nothing to do. There weren\xe2\x80\x99t even sports on television because all the games were cancelled. So there was nothing to bet on, and many people started individual investing.

\xe2\x80\x9cAs a pioneer in the field of behavioral finance, these must be pretty exciting times\xe2\x80\x9d is a very tactful way to say \xe2\x80\x9ceverything is pretty dumb now isn\xe2\x80\x99t it?\xe2\x80\x9d

NFT opportunity

Here\xe2\x80\x99s a story about a woman who made the only existing audio recording of J.D. Salinger, but who feels bad about it :

The author\xe2\x80\x99s memorabilia has proved to be a profitable market: In 1999 author Joyce Maynard sold 14 letters Salinger wrote to the software entrepreneur Peter Norton for $156,500; five years later, 41 letters Salinger had signed sold at Christie\xe2\x80\x99s for $185,000..

But Eppes has refused to sell the tape out of guilt over how she went about getting it. \xe2\x80\x9cIn the years after I did that, I came to regret it, terribly, terribly. I have spent many, many, many, many hours a day thinking about this. And, of course, it means an awful lot to me,\xe2\x80\x9d she says. \xe2\x80\x9cSometimes, I wake up in the middle of the night and I think, \xe2\x80\x98I stole that. I stole his voice.\xe2\x80\x99 You know that\xe2\x80\x99s like stealing somebody\xe2\x80\x99s soul, right? That tape is not mine to give or sell.\xe2\x80\x9d

Not long after our conversation, Eppes called me to let me know she\xe2\x80\x99d updated her will. The tape now will be placed, along with her body, in the crematorium.

Burning a unique cultural artifact? You know what that means! It\xe2\x80\x99s the \xe2\x80\x9c object-fire-token-money \xe2\x80\x9d NFT cycle : You can light a cultural object on fire, and then sell a non-fungible token \xe2\x80\x9cof\xe2\x80\x9d the former object on the blockchain. You can raise money off the twin demand for (1) Salinger memorabilia and (2) blockchain nonsense, and you don\xe2\x80\x99t even have to let anyone hear his voice. If nobody mints a non-fungible token of this recording then you\xe2\x80\x99ll know the NFT boom is truly over.

Good succession planning

Here is a story of, not so much a succession battle as a succession surprise , at $1.2 billion family business Scholastic Corp., the kids\xe2\x80\x99 book publisher that was an important part of my and many other people\xe2\x80\x99s childhoods and that\xc2\xa0publishes \xe2\x80\x9cHarry Potter.\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0Its longtime chief executive officer, M. Richard Robinson Jr., died in June and left control of the company to its chief strategy officer, Iole Lucchese. She has worked there for 30 years, ran the business in Canada and the entertainment division, pushed the company to expand its digital initiatives, and was a close adviser and strategic sparring partner for Robinson. She was also in a longtime romantic relationship with him. He left the company to her. Seems like a reasonable choice, putting the company in the hands of\xc2\xa0a person who knows it well and who has a good vision for its future?

On the other hand this means he did not leave the company to his two sons. The Wall Street Journal gives them some space to make their case for why they should run the company instead of the 30-year veteran chief strategy officer, and it is everything you could hope for:

[Younger son] Reece Robinson, [a 25-year-old filmmaker] who has done documentary work, said he tried to be involved with Scholastic but hasn\xe2\x80\x99t worked there full time. \xe2\x80\x9cI saw myself as an adviser,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cI didn\xe2\x80\x99t want to sacrifice my early 20s to work at a corporation.\xe2\x80\x9d

His brother Ben said he operates a sawmill and workshop that produces lumber, flooring and furniture from trees in Martha\xe2\x80\x99s Vineyard and lives off the land, noting in an email, \xe2\x80\x9cI fish the fish and cull the deer.\xe2\x80\x9d He also describes himself as a writer and \xe2\x80\x9cthe poet laureate who hasn\xe2\x80\x99t told his story yet.\xe2\x80\x9d

He roamed the Scholastic headquarters in New York City\xe2\x80\x99s SoHo neighborhood as a child, worked at the Scholastic store as a teenager and dressed up as \xe2\x80\x9cClifford\xe2\x80\x9d the dog for a parade.

In response to emailed questions, the elder son said his father \xe2\x80\x9cvalued my perspective in all matters, particularly regarding the trajectory of the company.\xe2\x80\x9d He said he told his father in recent years he wanted to be more involved in the company, including \xe2\x80\x9cmost explicitly my desire to be a member of the Board.\xe2\x80\x9d

The younger one didn\xe2\x80\x99t want to sacrifice his early 20s working at a corporation, but now that he\xe2\x80\x99s 25 he\xe2\x80\x99d like to run one. The older one lives off the land, but he could also use\xc2\xa0a board seat if one is available. I love them but I\xe2\x80\x99m glad Scholastic will be run by a grown-up.

Things happen

Tech Startup Financing Hits Records as Giant Funds Dwarf Venture Capitalists .\xc2\xa0Hedge funds enter private equity turf with deals for unlisted companies. Square to Buy Afterpay for $29 Billion to Tap Younger Users. Allianz Says U.S. Probe Into Funds Could Hurt Its Results. Fired Executive Says Deutsche Bank\xe2\x80\x99s DWS Overstated Sustainable-Investing Efforts . This 19-year-old earns $54,000 a year mining bitcoin as a full-time job \xe2\x80\x94 here\xe2\x80\x99s what it\xe2\x80\x99s like. \xe2\x80\x9cA representative for Penguin Random House said the publisher was unable to locate an employee who felt they had \xe2\x80\x98 the proper insight \xe2\x80\x99 to answer questions about this instance of bar code confusion.\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0Hamptons homeowners buying second houses for \xe2\x80\x98weekend getaways.\xe2\x80\x99\xc2\xa0Goose flying upside down is simply showing off, say experts .
